correlations.title: sleep fuels your energy
correlations.insight: on days following [sleepHours]+ hours of sleep, your energy averages [energyDiff] points higher.
correlations.explanation: sleep restores adenosine levels in the brain — adenosine buildup during waking hours creates the sensation of fatigue. 7-9 hours allows full clearance, resulting in higher subjective energy the next day.
correlations.recommendation: your sleep-energy link is clear. prioritizing 7+ hours tonight could lift tomorrow's energy.

correlations.title: sleep debt drains energy
correlations.insight: when your sleep drops below [threshold] hours, your energy falls by an average of [diff] points within [lag] days.
correlations.explanation: chronic sleep restriction creates cumulative cognitive impairment equivalent to total sleep deprivation. even 6 hours/night for 2 weeks produces deficits equal to 2 nights without sleep.
correlations.recommendation: you're accumulating sleep debt. even one recovery night of 8+ hours can partially restore energy levels.

correlations.title: yesterday's sleep shapes today's energy
correlations.insight: your energy today seems most influenced by last night's sleep. the effect carries over about 24 hours.
correlations.explanation: while same-day effects are strongest, sleep's impact on energy has a reliable next-day component. the body's recovery processes from sleep continue through the following day's first 12-16 hours.
correlations.recommendation: if you know tomorrow is a big day, prioritize tonight's sleep — the energy payoff comes within 24 hours.

correlations.title: consistent bedtime boosts energy more than sleep duration alone
correlations.insight: your energy may benefit from consistent sleep timing, not just more hours.
correlations.explanation: circadian rhythm regularity — going to bed and waking at the same time — improves sleep quality even at the same duration. irregular sleepers show 20-30% lower subjective energy than regular sleepers with identical total sleep time.
correlations.recommendation: try keeping your bedtime within a 30-minute window for the next 7 days. track whether your energy improves even if hours stay the same.

correlations.title: strategic naps can recover energy
correlations.insight: a 10-20 minute nap between 1-3pm can partially compensate for poor sleep.
correlations.explanation: short naps boost alertness for 2-3 hours without entering deep sleep (which causes grogginess). the post-lunch circadian dip makes this window ideal. longer naps (>30 min) risk sleep inertia.
correlations.recommendation: if last night was short, try a 15-minute nap before 3pm. set an alarm — sleeping longer backfires.

correlations.title: sleep and mood move together
correlations.insight: your mood tends to be [moodDiff] points higher on days after [sleepHours]+ hours of sleep.
correlations.explanation: sleep deprivation amplifies amygdala reactivity by up to 60%, making negative emotions feel stronger while blunting positive ones. adequate sleep restores prefrontal cortex regulation of emotional responses.
correlations.recommendation: the sleep-mood connection in your data is real. sleep is the foundation of emotional resilience.
correlations.source: Yoo et al. 2007; Nature Reviews Neuroscience

correlations.title: poor sleep, harder days
correlations.insight: nights under [threshold] hours tend to precede lower mood days for you.
correlations.explanation: the prefrontal cortex — responsible for emotional regulation — is among the first brain regions impaired by sleep loss. this creates a negativity bias where neutral events are perceived more negatively.
correlations.recommendation: when you notice a mood dip, check your recent sleep. the connection might be the explanation.
correlations.source: Walker & van der Helm 2009; Harvard Health

correlations.title: a wind-down routine protects both sleep and mood
correlations.insight: your mood-sleep pattern suggests a consistent wind-down routine could help both.
correlations.explanation: a 30-60 minute wind-down period (dim lights, no screens, calming activity) signals the brain to begin melatonin production. this improves both sleep onset latency and subjective sleep quality, which cascades into better next-day mood.
correlations.recommendation: tonight, try: dim lights at 9pm, no screens after 9:30pm, read or stretch until sleepy. track your mood tomorrow.

correlations.title: sleep's mood effect can lag a day
correlations.insight: one night of poor sleep may not crash your mood immediately, but two nights often does.
correlations.explanation: the brain can compensate for one night of poor sleep using cortisol reserves. by the second night, these reserves are depleted, and emotional regulation degrades noticeably. this creates a delayed effect.
correlations.recommendation: if you had one bad night, protect tonight's sleep especially — the mood impact compounds.

correlations.title: well-rested minds focus better
correlations.insight: your focus scores are [focusDiff] points higher after nights of [sleepHours]+ hours.
correlations.explanation: working memory capacity — essential for sustained focus — depends on sleep-dependent memory consolidation and prefrontal cortex restoration. even modest sleep restriction (6h vs 8h) measurably impairs attention.
correlations.recommendation: your data shows focus follows sleep closely. schedule demanding work after good sleep nights.

correlations.title: sleep loss fragments attention
correlations.insight: after nights under [threshold] hours, your focus drops by [diff] points on average.
correlations.explanation: sleep deprivation causes 'microsleeps' — brief lapses in attention lasting 1-10 seconds that you may not even notice. these accumulate throughout the day, making sustained concentration progressively harder.
correlations.recommendation: low focus today might trace back to last night's sleep. consider breaking work into shorter blocks.

correlations.title: schedule deep work after good sleep
correlations.insight: your focus peaks after 7+ hour sleep nights. plan accordingly.
correlations.explanation: prefrontal cortex function — which drives sustained attention and deep work — is most restored after adequate sleep. planning demanding cognitive tasks for good-sleep days and routine tasks for poor-sleep days optimizes your output.
correlations.recommendation: check your sleep before planning tomorrow. if it was good, schedule your hardest task first thing.

correlations.title: poor sleep triggers headaches
correlations.insight: your headaches appear [lagDays] day(s) after nights of less than [threshold] hours of sleep.
correlations.explanation: sleep deprivation lowers the pain threshold by reducing serotonin and increasing inflammatory markers. the connection often has a 24-48 hour delay as inflammatory processes build.
correlations.recommendation: if you see a sleep dip, preemptive hydration and stress management in the next 1-2 days may help prevent the headache.

correlations.title: oversleep and headaches
correlations.insight: headaches also appear on days after sleeping more than [threshold] hours.
correlations.explanation: oversleeping disrupts serotonin and other neurotransmitter rhythms. the resulting chemical imbalance can trigger headaches, particularly migraines. both too little and too much sleep are risk factors.
correlations.recommendation: consistency matters more than quantity. try to keep your sleep in the 7-9 hour range.

correlations.title: hydration buffer after poor sleep
correlations.insight: when you sleep poorly, preemptive hydration may prevent the headache that often follows.
correlations.explanation: dehydration and sleep deprivation compound each other in triggering headaches. the body loses water during sleep, and poor sleep disrupts hormones that regulate fluid balance.
correlations.recommendation: after a short sleep night, drink an extra 500ml of water before noon. track whether headaches still appear.

correlations.title: sleep affects your gut
correlations.insight: digestive issues tend to appear on days following poor sleep in your data.
correlations.explanation: the gut has its own circadian clock. sleep disruption alters gut motility, increases intestinal permeability ('leaky gut'), and shifts the microbiome composition within just 2 days of sleep restriction.
correlations.recommendation: your gut-sleep connection suggests prioritizing sleep could ease digestive discomfort.

correlations.title: gut-friendly sleep habits
correlations.insight: your digestion-sleep pattern suggests eating timing may matter.
correlations.explanation: eating within 2-3 hours of bedtime forces the gut to work during what should be rest mode, disrupting both sleep quality and digestive function. the gut's circadian clock expects a fasting period overnight.
correlations.recommendation: try finishing your last meal 3 hours before bed for the next week. track whether both sleep and digestion improve.
correlations.source: Benedict et al. 2016; de Oliveira & Burini 2009

correlations.title: the sleep-stress spiral
correlations.insight: in your data, poor sleep and high stress appear together frequently — each seems to amplify the other.
correlations.explanation: sleep deprivation raises cortisol (the stress hormone) by 37-45%. elevated cortisol then makes falling asleep harder, creating a vicious cycle. this is one of the most well-established bidirectional relationships in health science.
correlations.recommendation: breaking the cycle usually requires intervening on one side. try consistent sleep timing even when stressed.

correlations.title: break the stress-sleep cycle
correlations.insight: your data shows a stress-sleep spiral. breaking it on either end can help both.
correlations.explanation: the most effective intervention is a 'buffer zone' between the stressful day and sleep. journaling for 10 minutes before bed ('worry dump') reduces sleep onset latency by 9 minutes on average.
correlations.recommendation: tonight, try writing down everything on your mind for 10 minutes before bed. put the list aside — you've offloaded it.

correlations.title: consistent wake time anchors the cycle
correlations.insight: even when stressed, a consistent wake time can prevent the sleep-stress spiral from worsening.
correlations.explanation: the circadian system is anchored more by wake time than bedtime. keeping a consistent wake time (even on weekends) stabilizes cortisol rhythms, which reduces the stress-sleep feedback loop.
correlations.recommendation: set a fixed wake time and stick to it for 2 weeks, regardless of when you fall asleep. your stress and sleep patterns should both stabilize.

correlations.title: movement creates energy
correlations.insight: on days you exercise, your energy averages [diff] points higher.
correlations.explanation: counterintuitively, physical exertion increases energy. exercise boosts mitochondrial density (cellular energy production), increases cerebral blood flow, and triggers endorphin/endocannabinoid release. the effect lasts 12-24 hours.
correlations.recommendation: even 20 minutes of moderate activity could boost your energy. your data confirms the effect for you personally.

correlations.title: the minimum effective dose for energy
correlations.insight: you don't need a full workout — even a 10-minute walk boosts energy.
correlations.explanation: research shows a 10-minute brisk walk increases self-reported energy for up to 2 hours. the threshold for energy benefit is much lower than for cardiovascular fitness. intensity matters less than simply moving.
correlations.recommendation: on days you can't exercise, try a 10-minute walk instead of nothing. track whether even that small dose moves your energy score.
correlations.source: Puetz et al. 2006; WHO Physical Activity Guidelines

correlations.title: morning exercise yields all-day energy
correlations.insight: your data might show a timing pattern — morning exercise tends to boost energy more than evening.
correlations.explanation: morning exercise aligns with cortisol's natural peak, amplifying its energizing effects. it also sets the circadian clock for the day. evening exercise can boost energy too, but may interfere with sleep onset if done within 2 hours of bedtime.
correlations.recommendation: if possible, try moving your exercise earlier for a week and compare your energy scores to evening sessions.

correlations.title: exercise lifts mood
correlations.insight: your mood is [diff] points higher on exercise days compared to rest days.
correlations.explanation: exercise triggers release of endorphins, serotonin, dopamine, and BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor). a single session can elevate mood for 12-24 hours. regular exercise is as effective as SSRIs for mild-to-moderate depression.
correlations.recommendation: the exercise-mood boost is one of the strongest effects in your data. even a walk counts.
correlations.source: Chekroud et al. 2018; Frontiers in Psychology

correlations.title: social exercise doubles the mood benefit
correlations.insight: exercising with others may amplify the mood boost you're already seeing.
correlations.explanation: social exercise combines the neurochemical benefits of movement with oxytocin release from social connection. team sports, group classes, or even walking with a friend show stronger mood improvements than solo exercise in most studies.
correlations.recommendation: next time you exercise, try doing it with someone. compare your mood score to solo sessions.

correlations.title: outdoor exercise has bonus mood effects
correlations.insight: exercising outdoors may boost mood more than indoors — nature exposure adds its own benefit.
correlations.explanation: green exercise (physical activity in natural environments) shows additional mood improvements beyond indoor exercise. even 5 minutes of outdoor walking produces measurable mood improvement. sunlight exposure also helps regulate serotonin.
correlations.recommendation: if you usually exercise indoors, try one outdoor session this week and compare your mood score.

correlations.title: exercise improves sleep
correlations.insight: you tend to sleep [diff] hours longer on nights following exercise days.
correlations.explanation: exercise increases slow-wave sleep (the most restorative phase) and helps regulate circadian rhythm through body temperature changes. the effect is strongest with morning/afternoon exercise; late evening exercise can temporarily delay sleep onset.
correlations.recommendation: timing matters — your data might show whether morning or evening exercise helps your sleep more.

correlations.title: exercise timing affects sleep quality
correlations.insight: exercising more than 2 hours before bed tends to improve sleep; closer can delay it.
correlations.explanation: exercise raises core body temperature by 1-2°F. the subsequent cooling 1-2 hours later signals the brain to initiate sleep. exercising too close to bedtime keeps the body temperature elevated, delaying this signal.
correlations.recommendation: if you exercise in the evening, try finishing at least 2 hours before bed and see if your sleep improves.

correlations.title: movement sharpens focus
correlations.insight: your focus tends to be [diff] points higher on days you exercise.
correlations.explanation: exercise increases BDNF, which enhances neuroplasticity and cognitive function. it also increases blood flow to the prefrontal cortex. acute effects last 2-4 hours post-exercise; chronic effects build over weeks.
correlations.recommendation: scheduling exercise before your most demanding cognitive work could amplify focus.

correlations.title: exercise before deep work
correlations.insight: a 20-minute exercise session before focused work can extend concentration time.
correlations.explanation: the Naperville school study found that students who exercised before class showed dramatically improved academic performance. BDNF levels peak 30-60 minutes post-exercise, creating an ideal window for cognitive work.
correlations.recommendation: try a 20-minute workout before your most demanding task tomorrow. track whether your focus score improves.

correlations.title: exercise and headaches: it depends
correlations.insight: the exercise-headache relationship in your data is complex.
correlations.explanation: regular moderate exercise reduces headache frequency by 20-50% over time. however, intense exercise can trigger exertion headaches in susceptible individuals, especially when dehydrated. the key is consistency and hydration.
correlations.recommendation: track which types of exercise help vs. trigger headaches for you. intensity and hydration matter.
correlations.source: Varkey et al. 2011; American Migraine Foundation

correlations.title: hydrate before exercise to prevent headaches
correlations.insight: exercise-triggered headaches often come from dehydration, not the exercise itself.
correlations.explanation: dehydration reduces blood volume, forcing the heart to work harder. this can trigger headaches during or after exercise. drinking 500ml of water 30 minutes before exercise significantly reduces exertion headache risk.
correlations.recommendation: drink a full glass of water 30 minutes before your next workout. track whether the headache pattern changes.

correlations.title: exercise dissolves stress
correlations.insight: your stress levels are [diff] points lower on exercise days.
correlations.explanation: exercise reduces cortisol and adrenaline while stimulating endorphin production. it also activates the parasympathetic nervous system (rest-and-digest) post-exercise. the 'runner's high' is mediated by endocannabinoids, not just endorphins.
correlations.recommendation: when you notice stress building, even a 15-minute walk can reduce cortisol levels within 30 minutes.

correlations.title: exercise as a stress circuit breaker
correlations.insight: on high-stress days, even brief movement can break the cortisol cycle.
correlations.explanation: the stress response primes the body for physical action (fight-or-flight). actually completing that physical action — through exercise — signals the body that the threat has been handled, allowing cortisol to normalize. sedentary stress keeps cortisol elevated.
correlations.recommendation: next time stress spikes, try a 10-minute walk or 20 jumping jacks. track whether your stress score drops within the hour.

correlations.title: movement helps digestion
correlations.insight: you report fewer digestive issues on days you exercise.
correlations.explanation: moderate exercise increases gut motility (the speed food moves through your digestive system), reduces bloating, and promotes a healthier gut microbiome diversity. walking after meals is particularly effective.
correlations.recommendation: a post-meal walk might be especially helpful based on your digestion patterns.

correlations.title: a 15-minute post-meal walk aids digestion
correlations.insight: walking after eating may improve your digestion more than exercise at other times.
correlations.explanation: a short walk after meals accelerates gastric emptying by 30-50%, reducing bloating and discomfort. it also helps regulate blood sugar spikes from the meal. intense exercise after eating, however, can worsen symptoms.
correlations.recommendation: try a gentle 15-minute walk after your largest meal today. gentle — not intense.

correlations.title: stress drains your battery
correlations.insight: on high-stress days, your energy is [diff] points lower on average.
correlations.explanation: chronic stress keeps cortisol elevated, which diverts energy from normal metabolic processes to maintain the 'fight-or-flight' state. this is metabolically expensive — your body is burning energy on vigilance instead of vitality.
correlations.recommendation: stress-energy drain is one of your clear patterns. stress management could directly boost your energy.

correlations.title: micro-breaks prevent stress-related energy crashes
correlations.insight: taking short breaks on stressful days may prevent the energy crash your data shows.
correlations.explanation: the ultradian rhythm — a natural 90-120 minute cycle in alertness — means the body needs brief recovery periods. during stress, people often push through these cycles, accelerating energy depletion.
correlations.recommendation: set a timer for every 90 minutes on stressful days. take a 5-minute walk or stretch break. track whether your energy holds up better.

correlations.title: box breathing recovers energy during stress
correlations.insight: a 4-minute breathing exercise can shift your nervous system from stress to recovery mode.
correlations.explanation: box breathing (4 seconds inhale, 4 hold, 4 exhale, 4 hold) activates the vagus nerve, triggering the parasympathetic nervous system. this lowers cortisol within minutes, redirecting metabolic energy from stress to normal function.
correlations.recommendation: try box breathing for 4 minutes when you feel the stress-energy drain: inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s. repeat 4 cycles.

correlations.title: stress weighs on mood
correlations.insight: your mood drops by [diff] points on high-stress days.
correlations.explanation: chronic stress depletes serotonin and dopamine — the neurotransmitters responsible for positive mood. it also increases inflammatory cytokines which directly affect brain regions involved in mood regulation.
correlations.recommendation: this is bidirectional — low mood can also amplify stress perception. interrupting either one helps both.

correlations.title: one pleasant activity can break the stress-mood loop
correlations.insight: behavioral activation — doing one enjoyable thing — can interrupt the stress-mood downward spiral.
correlations.explanation: when stressed, people tend to withdraw from enjoyable activities, which worsens mood, which increases stress. deliberately scheduling one pleasant activity (even 15 minutes) breaks this cycle by providing a dopamine counterweight.
correlations.recommendation: on your next high-stress day, schedule 15 minutes of something you enjoy — music, a walk, cooking. non-negotiable.

correlations.title: stress fragments attention
correlations.insight: your focus drops by [diff] points when stress is elevated.
correlations.explanation: stress activates the amygdala which hijacks attention away from the prefrontal cortex (where focused work happens). the brain literally prioritizes threat scanning over deep concentration. this is adaptive for survival but terrible for knowledge work.
correlations.recommendation: on high-stress days, try shorter focus blocks (25 min) with breaks. your brain is in scanning mode.

correlations.title: pomodoro technique for stressed focus
correlations.insight: shorter work blocks can work with your brain's stress response instead of against it.
correlations.explanation: the stressed brain can sustain focus for about 25 minutes before the amygdala's threat-scanning pulls attention away. working in 25-minute blocks with 5-minute breaks accommodates this, yielding better total output than trying to force longer sessions.
correlations.recommendation: on high-stress days, use 25-minute focused blocks with 5-minute breaks. quality of focus often beats quantity of hours.

correlations.title: stress triggers headaches
correlations.insight: your headaches are [percent]% more likely on high-stress days.
correlations.explanation: tension-type headaches are directly caused by sustained muscle contraction in the head, neck, and shoulders during stress. stress also lowers the migraine threshold by depleting serotonin and increasing cortisol.
correlations.recommendation: shoulder and neck relaxation during stressful periods could help prevent the headache pattern.

correlations.title: neck stretches prevent stress headaches
correlations.insight: your stress-headache pattern suggests tension is building in your neck and shoulders.
correlations.explanation: tension headaches originate from sustained contraction of the trapezius and suboccipital muscles. a 2-minute stretch routine every hour during stressful work can prevent the tension from accumulating to headache threshold.
correlations.recommendation: set an hourly reminder on stressful days: roll your neck slowly 5 times each direction, then shrug and release your shoulders 3 times.

correlations.title: stress hits your gut
correlations.insight: digestive issues cluster around your high-stress periods.
correlations.explanation: the gut-brain axis is bidirectional — 95% of serotonin is produced in the gut. stress diverts blood flow away from the digestive system (fight-or-flight deprioritizes digestion), slows gut motility, and increases intestinal permeability.
correlations.recommendation: managing stress could be more effective for your digestion than dietary changes alone.

correlations.title: mindful eating on stressful days
correlations.insight: eating slowly during stressful periods may protect your digestion.
correlations.explanation: eating while stressed keeps the sympathetic nervous system active, suppressing digestive function. taking 3 deep breaths before eating activates the parasympathetic system ('rest and digest'), improving nutrient absorption and reducing GI symptoms.
correlations.recommendation: before your next meal on a stressful day: 3 slow deep breaths, then eat slowly for the first 5 minutes. track your digestion.

correlations.title: stress steals sleep
correlations.insight: you sleep [diff] hours less on nights following high-stress days.
correlations.explanation: cortisol and the sympathetic nervous system activation from stress directly inhibit melatonin production and maintain arousal. racing thoughts are the cognitive manifestation of this hyperarousal state.
correlations.recommendation: a wind-down buffer of 30-60 minutes before bed on stressful days can help transition to sleep.

correlations.title: mood and energy feed each other
correlations.insight: your mood and energy rise and fall together — [correlation] correlation.
correlations.explanation: mood and energy share neurochemical pathways — dopamine drives both motivation (energy) and reward (mood). when one drops, the other often follows. this bidirectional relationship means improving either one can lift both.
correlations.recommendation: when energy is low, a small mood boost (social interaction, music, sunlight) can lift both.

correlations.title: sunlight lifts both mood and energy
correlations.insight: 10 minutes of morning sunlight can boost the mood-energy connection you're seeing.
correlations.explanation: morning sunlight exposure suppresses melatonin, triggers cortisol (the healthy morning kind), and sets the circadian clock. these effects boost both alertness (energy) and serotonin production (mood) simultaneously.
correlations.recommendation: try 10 minutes of outdoor morning light within an hour of waking for the next 5 days. track both mood and energy.

correlations.title: better mood, better focus
correlations.insight: your focus is [diff] points higher on good-mood days.
correlations.explanation: positive mood broadens attention and increases cognitive flexibility (Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory). moderate positive mood enhances creative problem-solving, while very high arousal (excitement) can actually reduce focused attention.
correlations.recommendation: starting your day with something that lifts your mood could prime better focus for the whole day.

correlations.title: prime focus with a mood boost
correlations.insight: a brief mood-lifting activity before focused work may improve your concentration.
correlations.explanation: positive emotions broaden cognitive scope and increase working memory capacity. a 5-minute mood boost (favorite song, brief social interaction, gratitude reflection) can enhance subsequent focus for 30-60 minutes.
correlations.recommendation: before your next deep work session, listen to one favorite song or text someone you appreciate. then start working.

correlations.title: the gut-mood connection
correlations.insight: your mood and digestion patterns are linked in your data.
correlations.explanation: the enteric nervous system ('second brain') in your gut produces 95% of the body's serotonin and communicates directly with the brain via the vagus nerve. gut inflammation affects mood; mood affects gut motility. this is genuinely bidirectional.
correlations.recommendation: improving gut health (fiber, fermented foods, stress reduction) may lift mood, and vice versa.

correlations.title: probiotic foods may help both mood and gut
correlations.insight: the gut-mood link in your data suggests fermented foods could be worth trying.
correlations.explanation: fermented foods (yogurt, kimchi, kefir, sauerkraut) provide beneficial bacteria that produce neurotransmitter precursors in the gut. a Stanford study found 6 weeks of fermented food consumption reduced inflammatory markers and improved mood self-reports.
correlations.recommendation: try adding one serving of fermented food daily for 2 weeks. track both mood and digestion changes.

correlations.title: low mood and headaches cluster
correlations.insight: headaches and low mood appear together in your data.
correlations.explanation: both headaches and low mood involve serotonin dysregulation. migraines in particular share neurotransmitter pathways with depression — this is why certain antidepressants (tricyclics, SNRIs) are used to prevent migraines.
correlations.recommendation: treating one may help the other. focus on the upstream cause (often sleep or stress) that drives both.
correlations.source: Moore et al. 2013; American Migraine Foundation

correlations.title: headaches kill focus
correlations.insight: on headache days, your focus drops by [diff] points.
correlations.explanation: pain demands cognitive resources — the brain allocates attention to the pain signal, leaving less capacity for concentration. even mild headaches consume working memory bandwidth, reducing effective cognitive performance by 20-40%.
correlations.recommendation: addressing the root cause (sleep, stress, hydration) may be more effective than pushing through.
correlations.source: Moore et al. 2013; American Migraine Foundation

correlations.title: low energy days bring headaches
correlations.insight: headaches are [percent]% more common on your low-energy days.
correlations.explanation: low energy and headaches often share upstream causes: dehydration, poor sleep, stress, or blood sugar fluctuations. they're symptoms of the same underlying dysregulation rather than one causing the other.
correlations.recommendation: when energy is low, preemptive hydration and a small meal may prevent the associated headache.

correlations.title: blood sugar stability prevents both
correlations.insight: eating regularly may prevent both energy crashes and headaches.
correlations.explanation: blood sugar drops trigger both fatigue and headaches. the brain uses 20% of the body's glucose despite being only 2% of body weight. going more than 4-5 hours without eating can trigger both symptoms simultaneously.
correlations.recommendation: on days you notice low energy, check when you last ate. a small snack with protein and complex carbs may prevent a headache.

correlations.title: gut trouble drains energy
correlations.insight: your energy dips on days with digestive issues.
correlations.explanation: digestive discomfort activates inflammatory pathways that produce cytokines — signaling molecules that induce fatigue (this is why you feel tired when sick). even mild gut inflammation can cause 'sickness behavior' including reduced energy.
correlations.recommendation: the energy-digestion link in your data suggests gut health is an energy lever for you.

correlations.title: fiber for sustained energy and gut health
correlations.insight: increasing dietary fiber may help both your digestion and energy stability.
correlations.explanation: soluble fiber slows glucose absorption, preventing energy spikes and crashes. it also feeds beneficial gut bacteria that reduce inflammation. most adults eat only 15g/day of fiber vs. the recommended 25-30g.
correlations.recommendation: try adding one high-fiber food daily (beans, oats, berries, broccoli) for a week. track both energy and digestion.
correlations.source: Dantzer et al. 2008; WHO Nutrition Guidelines

correlations.title: gut issues and headaches cluster
correlations.insight: digestive issues and headaches tend to appear together in your data.
correlations.explanation: the gut-brain axis connects these directly. serotonin dysregulation affects both gut motility and migraine threshold. IBS and migraine have a 40-60% comorbidity rate, suggesting shared neurological pathways.
correlations.recommendation: these may share a root cause. track what you ate on days with both — food sensitivities could be a factor.
correlations.source: Cámara-Lemarroy et al. 2016; Nature Reviews Neuroscience

correlations.title: gut inflammation dampens mood
correlations.insight: your mood tends to be lower on days with digestive trouble.
correlations.explanation: gut inflammation produces cytokines that cross the blood-brain barrier and directly impact serotonin production in the brain. this 'inflammatory mood' effect explains why GI conditions are associated with higher rates of anxiety and depression.
correlations.recommendation: on days with digestive issues, be gentle with yourself — the mood impact is biological, not just about feeling uncomfortable.

correlations.title: stress and digestion are linked through the vagus nerve
correlations.insight: your digestive issues tend to worsen during stressful periods.
correlations.explanation: the vagus nerve — the longest cranial nerve — connects the brain directly to the gut. stress signals travel down this nerve and can cause cramping, slowed motility, and increased acid production within minutes of a stress trigger.
correlations.recommendation: deep breathing activates the vagus nerve's calming branch. try 5 slow breaths before meals on stressful days.

correlations.title: focus follows energy
correlations.insight: your focus and energy move together in your data.
correlations.explanation: sustained attention requires glucose and oxygen — both are more available when energy is high. the prefrontal cortex, which drives focus, is one of the most metabolically expensive brain regions. low energy = understaffed focus.
correlations.recommendation: if you need to focus, make sure your energy is supported first: eat, hydrate, and rest as needed.

correlations.title: stress steals focus resources
correlations.insight: your focus score is lower on high-stress days.
correlations.explanation: stress and focused attention compete for the same prefrontal cortex resources. under stress, the amygdala essentially commandeers the prefrontal cortex for threat monitoring, leaving less bandwidth for deliberate concentration.
correlations.recommendation: on high-stress days, reduce your focused work expectations and use shorter time blocks.

correlations.title: stress headaches often hit the next day
correlations.insight: your headaches sometimes appear the day after a high-stress day.
correlations.explanation: the 'let-down headache' phenomenon: during stress, cortisol and adrenaline mask pain. when stress drops (evening, weekend), the masking effect fades and accumulated muscle tension manifests as a headache. this explains weekend migraines.
correlations.recommendation: if you had a stressful day, gentle stretching in the evening may prevent tomorrow's headache.

correlations.title: exercise's mood boost can last 2 days
correlations.insight: you may notice higher mood not just on exercise days but the day after too.
correlations.explanation: while the acute endorphin boost fades within hours, BDNF elevation and anti-inflammatory effects from exercise persist for 24-48 hours. regular exercisers show persistent mood elevation between sessions.
correlations.recommendation: if you can't exercise every day, every-other-day may still keep your mood elevated continuously.

correlations.title: two bad nights compound focus loss
correlations.insight: one bad night reduces focus a little. two in a row reduces it a lot.
correlations.explanation: cognitive performance degrades linearly with cumulative sleep restriction. by the second day of 6-hour sleep, focus is impaired as much as staying awake for 24 hours straight. the effect compounds because the brain can't fully recover in one short night.
correlations.recommendation: if you've had two short nights, prioritize sleep tonight — the focus deficit is accumulating.

correlations.title: low mood disrupts sleep
correlations.insight: on low-mood days, your sleep quality tends to suffer.
correlations.explanation: negative mood states increase rumination — repetitive negative thinking — which is the #1 self-reported cause of difficulty falling asleep. low mood also reduces the motivation for good sleep hygiene behaviors.
correlations.recommendation: on low-mood evenings, try a brief gratitude exercise: write 3 things that went okay today. this reduces rumination and improves sleep onset.

correlations.title: high energy days are exercise days
correlations.insight: you're more likely to exercise on high-energy days — and exercise raises energy further.
correlations.explanation: this is a virtuous cycle: higher energy makes exercise feel easier, and exercise generates more energy. the key is to also exercise on low-energy days (even briefly) to prevent the opposite spiral.
correlations.recommendation: on low-energy days when exercise feels impossible, try just 5 minutes. most people find they continue once they start.

correlations.title: manage headaches to protect focus
correlations.insight: your focus drops significantly on headache days. early intervention helps both.
correlations.explanation: treating a headache early (within 30 minutes of onset) is far more effective than waiting. the pain-focus interference grows exponentially as the headache progresses, making early action the best strategy for both symptoms.
correlations.recommendation: at the first sign of a headache: hydrate, step away from screens for 5 minutes, and address the root cause (stress, posture, hunger).

correlations.title: sleep and exercise reinforce each other
correlations.insight: good sleep makes you more likely to exercise, and exercise improves your sleep.
correlations.explanation: this is one of the most powerful virtuous cycles in health. adequate sleep provides the energy and motivation for exercise. exercise increases slow-wave sleep depth. disrupting either can unravel both.
correlations.recommendation: when you notice this cycle breaking (poor sleep → no exercise → worse sleep), intervene on the easier side. usually that's exercise — even a short walk can restart the cycle.

correlations.title: gut discomfort disrupts concentration
correlations.insight: you tend to focus less well on days with digestive issues.
correlations.explanation: digestive discomfort activates interoceptive attention — the brain's monitoring of internal body signals. this diverts cognitive resources from external tasks, reducing available focus bandwidth.
correlations.recommendation: address digestive issues early in the day to protect your afternoon focus.
correlations.source: Mayer et al. 2015; Dantzer et al. 2008

correlations.title: sleep needs change with age
correlations.insight: your optimal sleep-energy ratio may shift over time.
correlations.explanation: adults under 25 often need 8-9 hours for peak energy. by 35-45, 7-8 hours is typical. over 55, sleep becomes lighter and more fragmented, but total need remains 7-8 hours. track your personal optimal.
correlations.recommendation: experiment with your sleep duration over a few weeks. your energy data will reveal your personal optimal range.

correlations.title: sleep quality matters as much as quantity
correlations.insight: 7 hours of uninterrupted sleep may beat 9 hours of fragmented sleep for your energy.
correlations.explanation: sleep efficiency (time asleep vs. time in bed) and sleep continuity affect next-day energy independently of total duration. frequent awakenings prevent the deep slow-wave sleep that most restores energy.
correlations.recommendation: if you sleep enough hours but still wake tired, focus on sleep quality: cool room, dark environment, consistent timing.

correlations.title: moderate intensity beats high intensity for energy
correlations.insight: your energy boost from exercise may depend on intensity.
correlations.explanation: moderate-intensity exercise (walking, cycling, yoga) consistently boosts energy. high-intensity exercise boosts energy after recovery but can cause short-term fatigue. the energy benefit peaks at moderate effort.
correlations.recommendation: if you're exercising for energy rather than fitness, keep intensity moderate. you should be able to hold a conversation.

correlations.title: acute stress energizes; chronic stress drains
correlations.insight: short bursts of stress can actually boost your energy temporarily.
correlations.explanation: acute stress triggers adrenaline, temporarily increasing alertness and energy (this is useful for deadlines). chronic stress, however, depletes cortisol reserves and causes persistent fatigue. the key is whether stress resolves.
correlations.recommendation: notice whether your stress has clear endpoints (healthy) or feels constant (needs intervention).

correlations.title: mood colors stress perception
correlations.insight: the same stressor feels harder when mood is already low.
correlations.explanation: low mood reduces the brain's capacity for cognitive reappraisal — the ability to reframe stressors positively. this means identical situations feel more threatening when mood is low, creating an amplification effect.
correlations.recommendation: when stress feels overwhelming, check your mood first. sometimes lifting mood (even slightly) makes the stressor more manageable.

correlations.title: sleep pattern changes trigger headaches more than sleep loss alone
correlations.insight: shifting your sleep schedule may trigger headaches even without reducing hours.
correlations.explanation: the brain adapts to consistent sleep timing. abrupt changes (weekend sleeping in, jet lag, shift work) disrupt neurotransmitter rhythms that regulate pain thresholds, even if total sleep is adequate.
correlations.recommendation: if headaches cluster around schedule changes (weekends, travel), focus on timing consistency rather than just duration.

correlations.title: rhythmic exercise is best for stress relief
correlations.insight: repetitive, rhythmic movements (walking, swimming, cycling) may reduce your stress more than variable-intensity exercise.
correlations.explanation: rhythmic exercise induces a meditative state through repetitive motion, activating the parasympathetic nervous system more effectively than start-stop activities. the rhythmic pattern also regulates breathing automatically.
correlations.recommendation: on high-stress days, choose rhythmic exercise: a steady walk, jog, or swim. the regularity of motion is part of the stress relief.

correlations.title: the hardest time to exercise is when it helps most
correlations.insight: low-mood days make exercise feel impossible, but those are the days it helps most.
correlations.explanation: low mood reduces motivation through dopamine depletion — the same system that drives the desire to move. this creates a paradox: exercise would restore dopamine, but you need dopamine to feel motivated to start.
correlations.recommendation: on low-mood days, commit to just 5 minutes. tell yourself you'll stop after 5 minutes if you want to. most people continue.

correlations.title: a little stress helps focus (yerkes-dodson law)
correlations.insight: moderate stress can actually sharpen your focus — it's the extremes that hurt.
correlations.explanation: the Yerkes-Dodson law describes an inverted-U relationship between stress and performance. too little stress = boredom, too much = anxiety. the sweet spot is moderate arousal — a deadline that feels challenging but achievable.
correlations.recommendation: notice your stress-focus sweet spot. if your focus data shows a peak at moderate stress, that's your productive zone.

correlations.title: late eating disrupts both gut and sleep
correlations.insight: eating close to bedtime may worsen both your digestion and sleep quality.
correlations.explanation: the gut's circadian clock expects a fasting period overnight. late meals force digestive activity during rest, which disrupts sleep architecture (especially REM sleep) and leads to poorer digestion of the late meal.
correlations.recommendation: try a 3-hour gap between your last meal and bedtime. your body will thank you on both fronts.

correlations.title: peak focus follows peak energy (usually morning)
correlations.insight: your best focus window probably aligns with your natural energy peak.
correlations.explanation: most people's cortisol peaks 30-60 minutes after waking, creating a natural focus window. a second smaller peak occurs in the late morning. the post-lunch dip (2-3pm) reliably reduces both energy and focus.
correlations.recommendation: schedule your most demanding cognitive work during your energy peak. save routine tasks for the post-lunch dip.

correlations.title: food sensitivities can trigger both headaches and gut issues
correlations.insight: when headaches and digestive issues appear together, food may be the common trigger.
correlations.explanation: certain foods (aged cheese, wine, chocolate, processed meats, artificial sweeteners) contain compounds that affect both the gut and the brain's pain pathways. these trigger both GI symptoms and headaches through shared serotonin pathways.
correlations.recommendation: when you log both a headache and digestive issue on the same day, note what you ate in the previous 24 hours. patterns may emerge.

correlations.title: REM sleep processes emotions
correlations.insight: the last 2 hours of an 8-hour sleep are mostly REM — the phase that processes emotions.
correlations.explanation: REM sleep strips the emotional charge from memories, helping you wake up with a more balanced perspective. cutting sleep to 6 hours eliminates most REM, meaning emotional memories remain 'raw' — contributing to mood instability.
correlations.recommendation: sleeping 8 hours instead of 6 doesn't just give you 33% more sleep — it gives you 2-3x more emotion-processing REM sleep.

correlations.title: exercise timing matters for digestion
correlations.insight: gentle exercise after meals helps digestion; intense exercise too soon after eating hurts it.
correlations.explanation: light walking after meals increases gut motility and blood flow to the digestive system. intense exercise, however, diverts blood away from the gut to muscles, which can cause cramping and nausea. timing and intensity both matter.
correlations.recommendation: for digestion: gentle walk within 30 minutes after eating. for intense exercise: wait 1.5-2 hours after a meal.

correlations.title: the 'let-down' headache on weekends
correlations.insight: do your headaches sometimes appear on weekends or after stressful periods end?
correlations.explanation: during stress, cortisol and adrenaline suppress pain perception. when stress resolves (weekends, vacations), these hormones drop rapidly, and accumulated muscle tension and vascular changes manifest as headaches.
correlations.recommendation: if you get weekend headaches, try to 'ramp down' stress gradually rather than going from max stress to full rest. a friday evening walk can help.

correlations.title: energy and mood: which comes first?
correlations.insight: your data shows energy and mood are tightly linked. they share the same upstream causes.
correlations.explanation: energy and mood are both outputs of the same neurochemical system (dopamine, serotonin). they move together because they share inputs: sleep, exercise, nutrition, stress. improving any input tends to lift both simultaneously.
correlations.recommendation: don't try to fix mood and energy separately — focus on their shared inputs (sleep, exercise, stress management).

correlations.title: the focus window after exercise
correlations.insight: your best focus window may be 30-120 minutes after exercise.
correlations.explanation: BDNF and blood flow to the prefrontal cortex peak 30-60 minutes post-exercise and remain elevated for about 2 hours. this creates an optimal window for deep cognitive work.
correlations.recommendation: experiment: exercise, wait 30 minutes, then start your most demanding task. track whether focus scores are higher on these days.

correlations.title: cortisol should be high in the morning, low at night
correlations.insight: healthy stress follows a daily rhythm — high cortisol on waking, low at bedtime.
correlations.explanation: cortisol follows a circadian pattern: high in the morning (wakes you up), declining through the day, lowest at bedtime (allows sleep). chronic stress flattens this curve — cortisol stays elevated at night, causing insomnia.
correlations.recommendation: morning sunlight amplifies the natural cortisol peak. evening relaxation helps cortisol drop. these restore the healthy rhythm.

correlations.title: caffeine withdrawal can cause both headaches and energy crashes
correlations.insight: if headaches and low energy appear together, caffeine withdrawal may be a factor.
correlations.explanation: regular caffeine users develop tolerance. on days with less caffeine (weekends, changes in routine), withdrawal can cause headaches and fatigue simultaneously. this is because caffeine blocks adenosine — withdrawal lets adenosine flood in.
correlations.recommendation: if headaches and energy dips align with caffeine changes, try keeping intake consistent or tapering gradually.

correlations.title: mood and headaches share a serotonin connection
correlations.insight: low mood and headaches may appear together because they share serotonin pathways.
correlations.explanation: serotonin regulates both mood and pain perception. when serotonin is depleted (from stress, poor sleep, or nutrition), both mood dips and headache threshold drops simultaneously. this is why some antidepressants also prevent migraines.
correlations.recommendation: when mood and headaches cluster, focus on serotonin-supporting behaviors: sleep, exercise, morning sunlight, and tryptophan-rich foods.
correlations.source: Moore et al. 2013; American Migraine Foundation

correlations.title: heavy meals reduce afternoon focus
correlations.insight: digestive load from large meals may explain your post-lunch focus dips.
correlations.explanation: digesting a large meal diverts blood to the gut and triggers parasympathetic activation (rest-and-digest). this combines with the natural circadian dip at 2-3pm, creating a focus trough. lighter lunches help.
correlations.recommendation: try a lighter lunch with protein and vegetables (not heavy carbs) and see if your afternoon focus improves.

correlations.title: the best exercise for stress depends on the type of stress
correlations.insight: mental stress responds best to physical exercise. physical stress responds best to rest.
correlations.explanation: for mental/emotional stress, exercise 'completes the stress cycle' by providing the physical output the fight-or-flight response prepared for. for physical stress (overwork, exhaustion), more physical activity can worsen things. match the intervention to the stressor.
correlations.recommendation: identify your stress type: if it's mental (worry, deadline, conflict), move your body. if it's physical (exhaustion, illness), rest.

correlations.title: dream sleep is emotion therapy
correlations.insight: adequate REM sleep literally processes your emotions overnight.
correlations.explanation: during REM sleep, the brain replays emotional memories without the stress hormones that accompanied the original experience. this 'overnight therapy' effect means you wake with a more balanced emotional perspective. truncated sleep = truncated emotional processing.
correlations.recommendation: the last sleep cycles are REM-heavy. sleeping 7.5-8 hours vs 6 hours gives disproportionately more emotional processing time.

correlations.title: stress burns energy even when you're sitting still
correlations.insight: high-stress sedentary days can be as draining as physically active days.
correlations.explanation: the stress response maintains elevated heart rate, blood pressure, and muscle tension — all of which burn glucose. mental stress can increase metabolic rate by 10-20%. this is why stressful desk days feel as exhausting as active ones.
correlations.recommendation: if you're drained after a sedentary stressful day, it's real fatigue — not laziness. your body was working hard on threat response.

correlations.title: regular exercise increases deep sleep by 20-30%
correlations.insight: consistent exercise improves sleep quality even more than sleep duration.
correlations.explanation: regular exercisers spend 20-30% more time in deep (slow-wave) sleep — the most physically restorative phase. this effect builds over 2-4 weeks of consistent exercise and is independent of exercise timing or intensity.
correlations.recommendation: exercise consistently for at least 2 weeks before judging its sleep impact. the deep sleep benefit takes time to build.

correlations.title: your microbiome affects your mood within days
correlations.insight: changes to your gut bacteria can shift your mood within 1-3 days.
correlations.explanation: the gut microbiome produces neurotransmitter precursors (serotonin, GABA, dopamine) that cross the blood-brain barrier. a diet shift can change microbiome composition within 24 hours, with mood effects following within 1-3 days.
correlations.recommendation: dietary changes affect mood faster than most people realize. if you change your diet, give it 3 days before checking the mood impact.

correlations.title: tired brains are more creative (but less focused)
correlations.insight: poor sleep reduces focused attention but may increase creative thinking.
correlations.explanation: sleep deprivation weakens the prefrontal cortex's inhibitory control, which is bad for logical focus but can allow more divergent, creative thinking. this is why insights often come when tired. but implementing those insights requires focused energy.
correlations.recommendation: use your tired moments for brainstorming and your rested moments for execution.

correlations.title: regular exercise prevents headaches long-term
correlations.insight: consistent exercise reduces headache frequency by 20-50% over weeks.
correlations.explanation: while individual exercise sessions can sometimes trigger headaches (especially intense ones), regular moderate exercise reduces headache frequency significantly over 6-12 weeks. the mechanism involves improved vascular health and reduced inflammatory markers.
correlations.recommendation: if you get frequent headaches, 3-4 weekly sessions of moderate exercise may be one of the best preventive strategies.

correlations.title: resilience is a skill, not a trait
correlations.insight: your ability to maintain mood during stress can improve with practice.
correlations.explanation: stress resilience depends on prefrontal cortex function, social support, and practiced coping strategies. people who regularly practice stress management show smaller mood drops during stressful periods. the brain's stress response is trainable.
correlations.recommendation: consistent small stress management practices (breathing, walks, social connection) build resilience over weeks. it's like training a muscle.

recommendations.title: tonight's sleep = tomorrow's energy
recommendations.action: dim lights by 9pm. no screens after 9:30pm. aim for 7.5+ hours.
recommendations.rationale: your data shows energy drops significantly after short sleep nights. tonight's sleep directly predicts tomorrow's energy level.

recommendations.title: movement is the fastest mood lever
recommendations.action: 20 minutes of any movement today — walk, stretch, dance. even 10 minutes helps.
recommendations.rationale: 2+ days of below-average mood in your data. exercise produces measurable mood improvement within 30 minutes and lasts 12-24 hours.

recommendations.title: activate your calm system
recommendations.action: box breathing: inhale 4 seconds, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4. repeat 4 times. takes under 2 minutes.
recommendations.rationale: stress has been elevated. box breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system within minutes, lowering cortisol and heart rate.

recommendations.title: hydration check
recommendations.action: drink 500ml of water now if you haven't had much today. dehydration is the #1 preventable headache trigger.
recommendations.rationale: headache reported. 75% of headaches have a dehydration component. hydrating is the lowest-risk, highest-return intervention.

recommendations.title: consistency beats quantity
recommendations.action: pick a fixed bedtime and wake time. keep it within a 30-minute window for the next week.
recommendations.rationale: your sleep timing has been variable. irregular sleep schedules impair circadian rhythm function, reducing sleep quality even when hours are adequate.

recommendations.title: 3 days without movement
recommendations.action: even a 10-minute walk counts. the energy-exercise virtuous cycle breaks after 2-3 sedentary days.
recommendations.rationale: 3 days without exercise. research shows the energy and mood benefits of exercise fade after 48-72 hours of inactivity.

recommendations.title: focus follows sleep
recommendations.action: check your recent sleep — 2+ days of low focus often traces back to accumulated sleep debt. prioritize tonight's sleep.
recommendations.rationale: 2+ days of below-average focus. the most common upstream cause is sleep restriction, which compounds over consecutive nights.

recommendations.title: your gut feels your stress
recommendations.action: 3 deep breaths before your next meal. eat slowly for the first 5 minutes. this activates your digestive system.
recommendations.rationale: digestive issues coincide with stress in your data. the vagus nerve connects stress directly to gut function.

recommendations.title: morning light reset
recommendations.action: get 10 minutes of outdoor morning light within an hour of waking. this sets your circadian clock and boosts serotonin.
recommendations.rationale: 3+ days of below-average mood. morning light exposure is one of the most evidence-based non-pharmacological mood interventions.

recommendations.title: social connection buffers stress
recommendations.action: reach out to one person today — a text, a call, coffee. social connection reduces cortisol even in brief interactions.
recommendations.rationale: 3+ days of elevated stress. social isolation amplifies the stress response; brief social contact activates oxytocin pathways that buffer cortisol.

recommendations.title: check your fuel
recommendations.action: ensure you're eating every 4-5 hours with protein at each meal. blood sugar stability is a key energy driver.
recommendations.rationale: 3+ days of low energy. after ruling out sleep issues, nutrition is the next most common cause of persistent fatigue.

recommendations.title: check your posture and screen setup
recommendations.action: eyes should be level with the top third of your screen. shoulders back and down. take a screen break every 30 minutes.
recommendations.rationale: recurring headaches often have a posture component, especially for desk workers. screen distance and angle directly affect neck tension.

recommendations.title: weekend sleep binge = social jet lag
recommendations.action: try to keep weekend sleep within 1 hour of weekday timing. catching up disrupts your circadian rhythm.
recommendations.rationale: sleeping significantly more on weekends indicates weekday sleep debt. this 'social jet lag' effect can worsen monday mood and energy.

recommendations.title: exercise + headache? check intensity and hydration
recommendations.action: make sure you're hydrated before exercise. if headaches persist with intense exercise, try moderate intensity instead.
recommendations.rationale: exercise-triggered headaches are usually caused by dehydration or sudden intensity increases, not exercise itself.

recommendations.title: a tiny gratitude practice
recommendations.action: write down 3 things that went okay today. doesn't have to be profound — 'had good coffee' counts.
recommendations.rationale: gratitude exercises have measurable effects on mood neurotransmitters. even brief practices shift attention from threat scanning to positive appraisal.

recommendations.title: check your environment
recommendations.action: phone on silent in another room. close unnecessary tabs. clear your desk surface. small environmental changes have outsized focus effects.
recommendations.rationale: environmental distractions are often the most immediate and fixable cause of poor focus, even more than sleep or stress.

recommendations.title: cold water wake-up
recommendations.action: splash cold water on your face or end your shower with 30 seconds of cold water. the shock activates your sympathetic nervous system.
recommendations.rationale: cold exposure triggers norepinephrine release, which increases alertness. the effect is immediate and lasts about 30-60 minutes.

recommendations.title: check your caffeine cutoff time
recommendations.action: no caffeine after 2pm. caffeine's half-life is 5-6 hours — an afternoon coffee still has 25% left at midnight.
recommendations.rationale: poor sleep nights may correlate with late caffeine consumption. caffeine blocks adenosine receptors, directly preventing sleep pressure from building.

recommendations.title: 5 minutes in nature
recommendations.action: step outside for 5 minutes. look at trees, sky, or water. nature exposure reduces cortisol within 10 minutes.
recommendations.rationale: attention restoration theory: natural environments require less directed attention than urban environments, allowing the stress-attention system to recover.

recommendations.title: increase fiber gradually
recommendations.action: add one serving of high-fiber food today: oats, beans, berries, or broccoli. increase gradually over a week.
recommendations.rationale: recurring digestive issues may indicate insufficient fiber intake. most adults eat half the recommended amount. increase slowly to avoid gas.

recommendations.title: keep weekend timing close to weekday
recommendations.action: try waking within 1 hour of your weekday alarm on weekends.
recommendations.rationale: 'social jet lag' from weekend sleep-ins disrupts circadian rhythm and causes monday grogginess.

recommendations.title: reach out to someone
recommendations.action: text or call one person you enjoy talking to. brief social connection boosts mood for hours.
recommendations.rationale: social isolation amplifies mood dips. even a 5-minute positive interaction activates oxytocin and buffers cortisol.

recommendations.title: protein at breakfast stabilizes morning energy
recommendations.action: include 20g+ protein at breakfast (eggs, yogurt, nuts). prevents the mid-morning crash from high-carb breakfasts.
recommendations.rationale: protein slows glucose absorption, preventing the insulin spike and subsequent crash that causes mid-morning fatigue.

recommendations.title: phone in another room
recommendations.action: put your phone in another room during focused work. just having it visible reduces cognitive capacity by 10%.
recommendations.rationale: the mere presence of a smartphone reduces available cognitive capacity, even when the phone is face-down and on silent.
recommendations.source: Ward et al. 2017; Mark et al. 2008

recommendations.title: track your water intake today
recommendations.action: aim for 2-3 liters of water. headaches and dehydration are so linked that hydration should be the first intervention.
recommendations.rationale: mild dehydration (1-3% body weight loss) is common and causes headaches, fatigue, and reduced focus — all reversible with hydration.

recommendations.title: gentle walk after meals
recommendations.action: a 10-15 minute walk after your largest meal can speed digestion by 30-50%.
recommendations.rationale: walking accelerates gastric emptying and reduces bloating through gentle mechanical movement.

recommendations.title: worry dump before bed
recommendations.action: spend 10 minutes writing everything on your mind before bed. offload the thoughts so your brain doesn't replay them at 3am.
recommendations.rationale: constructive worry journaling reduces sleep onset latency by an average of 9 minutes and improves subjective sleep quality.

recommendations.title: 3-day exercise streak! keep it going
recommendations.action: you've exercised 3 days in a row. tomorrow is the critical day — most streaks break on day 4. just 10 minutes counts.
recommendations.rationale: behavioral research shows the habit-forming window for exercise is 3-6 consecutive days. protecting the streak through the first week dramatically increases long-term adherence.

recommendations.title: check your room temperature
recommendations.action: ideal sleep temperature is 65-68°F (18-20°C). a cool room is one of the most underrated sleep interventions.
recommendations.rationale: core body temperature needs to drop 1-2°F to initiate sleep. a cool room facilitates this natural temperature decline.

recommendations.title: movement snack
recommendations.action: stand up and stretch for 2 minutes. or 10 jumping jacks. micro-doses of movement shift mood measurably.
recommendations.rationale: even 2-minute 'movement snacks' trigger small dopamine releases that shift mood. the effect is modest but immediate.

recommendations.title: the 2pm dip is normal
recommendations.action: the post-lunch energy dip is circadian, not personal failure. try: 10-minute walk, splash cold water on your face, or a 15-minute nap (not longer).
recommendations.rationale: everyone has a circadian energy dip around 2-3pm. acknowledging it as normal (not fixable by caffeine alone) allows better strategies.

recommendations.title: reduce decision load
recommendations.action: on stressful days, reduce unnecessary decisions: plan meals in advance, lay out clothes, batch similar tasks.
recommendations.rationale: decision fatigue compounds stress. each decision uses the same cognitive resources as stress management. reducing decisions frees resources for stress coping.

recommendations.title: manage your light exposure
recommendations.action: bright light in the morning (go outside for 10 min). dim, warm light in the evening. blue light from screens suppresses melatonin.
recommendations.rationale: light is the primary zeitgeber (time-giver) for the circadian clock. strategic light exposure is more effective than melatonin supplements.

recommendations.title: take a screen break
recommendations.action: step away from screens for 10 minutes. look at something far away. screen-related eye strain is a top headache trigger for desk workers.
recommendations.rationale: prolonged near-focus causes ciliary muscle strain, which triggers tension headaches. the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds) helps prevent this.

recommendations.title: single-task for one hour
recommendations.action: pick one task. close everything else. set a 60-minute timer. multitasking reduces effective IQ by 10 points.
recommendations.rationale: context switching costs 15-25 minutes of recovery per switch. single-tasking for even one hour produces more output than 3 hours of multitasking.

recommendations.title: alcohol fragments sleep
recommendations.action: if you drank last night and slept poorly, that's the connection. alcohol suppresses REM sleep and causes 2nd-half-of-night awakenings.
recommendations.rationale: alcohol is sedating but not sleep-promoting. it blocks REM sleep and causes fragmented sleep after blood alcohol drops.

recommendations.title: check hydration first
recommendations.action: drink 500ml of water. mild dehydration (which you may not feel as thirst) is the most common and fixable cause of low energy.
recommendations.rationale: even 1-2% dehydration reduces cognitive performance and energy. most people don't drink enough water.

recommendations.title: your mood may be a sleep problem
recommendations.action: your mood and sleep are both low. prioritize sleep tonight — mood often auto-corrects after a good night's rest.
recommendations.rationale: 60% of mood variability in the general population can be explained by sleep quality. fix sleep first, then reassess mood.

recommendations.title: respect your focus limit
recommendations.action: most people max out at 4 hours of deep focus per day. if you've been pushing beyond that, the declining trend is expected.
recommendations.rationale: deep focus depletes glucose and neurotransmitters. after ~4 hours, returns diminish. rest is more productive than pushing through.

recommendations.title: complete the stress cycle with movement
recommendations.action: stress primes your body for physical action. completing that action (a walk, stretches, even shaking it out) tells your body the threat has passed.
recommendations.rationale: the stress cycle: trigger → fight/flight preparation → physical action → recovery. skipping the physical action leaves cortisol elevated.

recommendations.title: peppermint tea for digestive discomfort
recommendations.action: peppermint relaxes smooth muscle in the digestive tract. a cup of peppermint tea after meals can reduce bloating and cramping.
recommendations.rationale: peppermint has antispasmodic properties supported by clinical trials for IBS symptoms.

recommendations.title: 5 days without movement
recommendations.action: your body needs movement. today: commit to just 5 minutes. literally 5 minutes of walking, stretching, or stairs. that's it.
recommendations.rationale: 5 days of inactivity begins measurable cardiovascular and metabolic decline. the minimum effective dose is lower than you think — even 5 minutes has measurable benefits.

recommendations.title: consider magnesium
recommendations.action: 400mg of magnesium glycinate before bed. magnesium deficiency is common and linked to both headaches and poor sleep.
recommendations.rationale: 50% of migraine patients are magnesium deficient. supplementation reduces migraine frequency by 41% in clinical trials.

recommendations.title: gratitude journaling improves sleep
recommendations.action: write 3 things you're grateful for before bed. this simple practice improves sleep quality and reduces pre-sleep worry.
recommendations.rationale: gratitude journaling reduces negative pre-sleep cognition, which is the #1 cause of difficulty falling asleep.
recommendations.source: Wood et al. 2009; Scullin et al. 2018

recommendations.title: do one small thing
recommendations.action: complete one tiny task: make your bed, wash a dish, reply to one email. the sense of accomplishment, however small, activates reward circuits.
recommendations.rationale: completing tasks releases dopamine regardless of task size. when mood is low, tiny wins build momentum for bigger ones.

recommendations.title: check your posture
recommendations.action: sit up straight or stand for a minute. slouching compresses your diaphragm, reducing oxygen intake and increasing fatigue.
recommendations.rationale: posture affects breathing mechanics and blood flow. an upright posture increases alertness through both mechanical and psychological pathways.

recommendations.title: progressive muscle relaxation (2 min)
recommendations.action: tense each muscle group for 5 seconds, then release: feet, calves, thighs, abs, hands, arms, shoulders, face. takes 2 minutes.
recommendations.rationale: progressive muscle relaxation teaches the body to recognize and release tension, reducing cortisol and muscle-related symptoms (headache, neck pain).

recommendations.title: strategic caffeine timing
recommendations.action: wait 90 minutes after waking before caffeine (let natural cortisol peak first). then use caffeine strategically before your hardest task.
recommendations.rationale: caffeine blocks adenosine, not cortisol. using it when cortisol is already high wastes its effect. timing caffeine for your productive window maximizes impact.

recommendations.title: 7-day streak! you've unlocked your first connections
recommendations.action: you've logged 7 consecutive days. your personal correlation map is now active. check your network for new connections.
recommendations.rationale: 7 days is the minimum for statistically meaningful correlations. your data is now telling a story.

recommendations.title: 30 days. your body map is real.
recommendations.action: one month of data. your correlations are now reliable, your predictions are improving, and your patterns are uniquely yours.
recommendations.rationale: 30 days provides enough data for robust trend detection, cycle identification, and reliable correlation coefficients.

recommendations.title: morning light resets your clock
recommendations.action: get 10 minutes of bright light (ideally outdoor sunlight) within 30 minutes of waking. this is the single most powerful circadian signal.
recommendations.rationale: below-average energy often correlates with circadian misalignment. morning light exposure advances your body clock and boosts cortisol (the wake-up hormone) at the right time.

recommendations.title: hydrate first
recommendations.action: drink 500ml of water right now. dehydration is the most underdiagnosed headache trigger — 75% of people are chronically mildly dehydrated.
recommendations.rationale: headache reported today. dehydration-related headaches respond to fluid intake within 30-60 minutes without medication.
recommendations.source: Spigt et al. 2012; European Journal of Neurology

recommendations.title: create a 30-minute wind-down
recommendations.action: starting tonight: set a phone alarm 30 minutes before your target bedtime. dim lights, no screens, do something calming (read, stretch, breathe). same routine every night.
recommendations.rationale: your recent sleep has been short. a consistent pre-sleep ritual trains your brain to associate these cues with sleep onset, reducing time to fall asleep by 15-20 minutes on average.
recommendations.source: Irish et al. 2015; Sleep Health journal

recommendations.title: a 10-minute post-meal walk helps
recommendations.action: walk for 10-15 minutes after your largest meal today. even a slow walk accelerates gastric emptying and reduces bloating.
recommendations.rationale: digestive discomfort reported. post-meal walking reduces blood sugar spikes by 30% and improves gut motility immediately.

recommendations.title: try 25-minute focus blocks
recommendations.action: work in 25-minute sprints with 5-minute breaks (Pomodoro technique). for the next task, set a timer and commit to 25 minutes of single-tasking.
recommendations.rationale: focus has been low recently. the Pomodoro technique works because 25 minutes is within most people's sustainable attention span, and the timer creates external accountability.

baselines.title: sleep: what's normal
baselines.description: adults need 7-9 hours. the CDC reports the average american gets 6.8 hours. anything under 7 consistently is classified as short sleep.
baselines.ageVariation: younger adults (18-25) may need up to 9 hours. adults over 65 often need 7-8 hours but get less deep sleep.

baselines.title: energy: what's normal
baselines.description: most people report moderate energy (3/5) on an average day. natural dips occur at 2-3pm (post-lunch circadian dip) and recover by 4-5pm.
baselines.ageVariation: energy levels are highest in the late teens/early 20s and gradually decline. however, regular exercise can offset age-related energy decline.

baselines.title: mood: what's normal
baselines.description: most people rate their mood around 3.5/5 on a typical day. mild fluctuations are normal. persistent scores below 2 for more than 2 weeks may indicate clinical depression.
baselines.ageVariation: mood tends to be lowest in the late 40s ('midlife dip') and improves again after 50. seasonal variation is common — lower in winter, higher in summer.

baselines.title: focus: what's normal
baselines.description: sustained attention naturally fluctuates in 90-minute cycles. peak focus periods typically last 20-40 minutes before requiring a break. most people sustain about 4 hours of truly focused work per day.
baselines.ageVariation: focus ability matures into the mid-20s (prefrontal cortex development). it remains stable through middle age, then gradually declines. regular cognitive challenges slow the decline.

baselines.title: headaches: what's normal
baselines.description: about 50% of adults experience at least one headache per month. tension-type headaches are most common. more than 15 headache days per month is classified as chronic daily headache.
baselines.ageVariation: headache frequency peaks in the 30s-40s and often decreases after 55. women experience more headaches than men (hormonal factors).

baselines.title: digestion: what's normal
baselines.description: occasional digestive discomfort (1-2 days/week) is common. frequent issues (4+ days/week) may indicate IBS or food sensitivities. the gut takes 24-72 hours to process food, so symptoms often lag meals.
baselines.ageVariation: digestive issues tend to increase with age due to reduced gut motility and stomach acid production. however, diet and exercise have stronger effects than age.

baselines.title: stress: what's normal
baselines.description: some stress is normal and even beneficial (eustress). the average adult reports moderate stress. persistent levels above 4/5 indicate chronic stress, which has measurable health consequences.
baselines.ageVariation: stress peaks during the 25-44 age range (career building, family responsibilities). it often decreases after 55. however, individual variation is huge — circumstances matter more than age.

baselines.title: exercise: what's recommended
baselines.description: WHO recommends 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week (about 30 min/day, 5 days/week). only 23% of adults meet this guideline. any exercise is better than none — even 10 minutes has measurable benefits.
baselines.ageVariation: exercise needs don't decrease with age — if anything, they increase. resistance training becomes more important after 40 to maintain muscle mass and bone density.

education.title: why track sleep
education.content: sleep is the single most impactful signal on your health. it affects energy, mood, focus, immune function, and even digestion. most people underestimate their sleep debt because they've adapted to feeling tired.
education.funFact: after 17 hours awake, cognitive performance equals a blood alcohol level of 0.05% — legally impaired in many countries.

education.title: why track energy
education.content: energy isn't random — it follows patterns you can decode. sleep, exercise, stress, nutrition, and circadian rhythm all contribute. tracking reveals your personal energy formula.
education.funFact: the brain uses 20% of your body's energy despite being only 2% of your body weight. when energy is low, cognitive function drops first.

education.title: why track mood
