education.content: mood isn't just 'how you feel' — it's a signal about your body's state. sleep, gut health, exercise, and social connection all affect mood through measurable neurochemical pathways.
education.funFact: 95% of your serotonin (the 'happiness molecule') is produced in your gut, not your brain. your mood is partly a gut feeling — literally.

education.title: why track focus
education.content: sustained focus is a limited resource that depletes and recovers. tracking reveals what depletes your focus fastest and what restores it. most people can only sustain deep focus for about 4 hours per day.
education.funFact: it takes an average of 23 minutes to return to deep focus after a distraction. a single phone notification can cost you nearly half an hour of productive work.

education.title: why track headaches
education.content: headaches are rarely random — they have triggers. sleep, stress, dehydration, and posture are the most common. tracking creates a personal trigger map that can reduce frequency by 30-50%.
education.funFact: the brain itself can't feel pain — it has no pain receptors. headaches come from blood vessels, muscles, and nerves surrounding the brain.

education.title: why track digestion
education.content: your gut is your 'second brain' with 500 million neurons. digestive health affects mood, energy, sleep, and immune function. tracking helps you identify foods and patterns that trigger problems.
education.funFact: your gut microbiome weighs about 2kg (4.4 lbs) and contains more cells than the rest of your body combined. it changes measurably within 24 hours of a diet change.

education.title: why track stress
education.content: stress isn't just a feeling — it's a physiological state that affects every system in your body. tracking reveals your stress patterns: when it spikes, what triggers it, and what resolves it.
education.funFact: short-term stress actually enhances immune function and cognitive performance ('eustress'). it's chronic, unresolved stress that causes damage. the dose makes the poison.

education.title: why track exercise
education.content: exercise is the closest thing to a universal health intervention. it improves sleep, mood, energy, focus, and digestion — often simultaneously. tracking reveals which exercise patterns give you the biggest personal benefit.
education.funFact: a single 30-minute exercise session produces measurable improvements in mood for up to 24 hours. regular exercise is as effective as antidepressants for mild-to-moderate depression.
education.source: Chekroud et al. 2018; Puetz et al. 2006

education.title: what happens while you sleep
education.content: your brain cycles through 4-5 sleep cycles per night (~90 min each). early cycles are dominated by deep slow-wave sleep (physical repair, growth hormone release). later cycles are REM-heavy (memory consolidation, emotional processing). cutting sleep short steals REM time specifically.
education.funFact: your brain uses almost as much energy during REM sleep as when you're awake.
education.source: Walker 2017, Why We Sleep; National Sleep Foundation

education.title: your natural energy curve
education.content: energy isn't flat throughout the day. most people peak around 10-11am, dip at 1-3pm (the post-lunch circadian trough — it happens even without eating), and have a second smaller peak around 6pm. knowing your curve helps you schedule demanding tasks wisely.
education.funFact: the afternoon dip is biological, not just from food. it's a mini version of your nighttime sleep drive.

education.title: mood isn't just 'feeling good'
education.content: mood reflects a complex interplay of serotonin (stability), dopamine (motivation/reward), norepinephrine (alertness), and GABA (calm). exercise, sleep, sunlight, and social connection each uniquely influence different neurotransmitter systems — which is why tracking multiple signals reveals which levers work for you.
education.funFact: about 95% of your serotonin is produced in your gut, not your brain — hence the gut-mood connection.

education.title: focus isn't one thing
education.content: cognitive science identifies at least 3 types of attention: sustained (staying on task), selective (filtering distractions), and executive (switching between tasks). sleep deprivation impairs sustained attention first. stress impairs executive attention. understanding which type is suffering helps target the fix.
education.funFact: your brain can only maintain peak focus for about 90 minutes before needing a break — matching the ultradian rhythm.

education.title: stress accumulates like debt
education.content: short-term stress can be beneficial (sharpens focus, boosts immunity briefly). but chronic stress creates 'allostatic load' — wear on the body that accumulates over weeks and months. tracking stress daily helps you catch accumulation before it manifests as illness, insomnia, or burnout.
education.funFact: chronic stress literally shrinks the prefrontal cortex (decision-making) while enlarging the amygdala (threat detection).

education.title: exercise grows your brain
education.content: aerobic exercise triggers release of BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), which promotes new neuron growth in the hippocampus — the brain region critical for memory and emotional regulation. a single workout produces measurable BDNF increase within 30 minutes.
education.funFact: exercise has been called 'Miracle-Gro for the brain' by neuroscientist John Ratey. it's the single most effective non-pharmaceutical intervention for mood and cognition.

education.title: headaches are multifactorial
education.content: tension headaches and migraines have different trigger profiles, but both share common aggravators: dehydration, poor sleep, high stress, bright light, and irregular meals. tracking headaches alongside other signals reveals YOUR specific trigger pattern — most people have 2-3 primary triggers they can manage.
education.funFact: migraines affect 12% of the population and are 3x more common in women — they're not 'just headaches' but a neurological condition.
education.source: American Migraine Foundation; Buse et al. 2019

education.title: your gut talks to your brain
education.content: the gut-brain axis is a bidirectional communication highway via the vagus nerve. stress slows digestion (fight-or-flight diverts blood from gut). poor sleep disrupts the gut microbiome within 2 days. exercise improves gut motility and microbiome diversity. tracking digestion alongside these signals reveals the connections.
education.funFact: your gut has its own nervous system with 100 million neurons — sometimes called the 'second brain.'
education.source: Mayer 2011; Carabotti et al. 2015; Gut Journal

coldStart.title: most people find sleep is their #1 energy lever
coldStart.content: research across millions of people shows that sleep duration is the strongest predictor of next-day energy. each hour below 7 reduces self-reported energy by about 15-20%. you'll discover your personal threshold soon.

coldStart.title: exercise → mood is one of the strongest connections in health science
coldStart.content: a study of 1.2 million people found that exercise reduces mental health burden by 43%. even a single session elevates mood for 12-24 hours. your personal data will show exactly how strong this effect is for you.

coldStart.title: the stress-sleep spiral is real
coldStart.content: stress raises cortisol by 37-45%, which blocks melatonin production. poor sleep then raises stress the next day. most people don't realize how directly these two are connected until they track both.

coldStart.title: your gut and your mood share a direct line
coldStart.content: 95% of serotonin is made in the gut. the vagus nerve connects your gut directly to your brain. gut inflammation can lower mood within hours. tracking both reveals a pattern most people never notice.

coldStart.title: sleep debt accumulates invisibly
coldStart.content: after 2 weeks of 6-hour nights, your cognitive performance equals someone who has been awake for 48 hours straight — but you won't feel that impaired. tracking makes the invisible visible.

coldStart.title: the exercise-energy paradox
coldStart.content: spending energy on exercise creates more energy — across 70 studies, regular exercise reduced fatigue by 20% and increased energy by 65%. this counterintuitive effect will likely show up in your data.

coldStart.title: most headaches have predictable triggers
coldStart.content: both too little and too much sleep increase headache risk. stress, dehydration, and skipped meals are the other top triggers. with enough data, meridian can identify your personal trigger pattern.

coldStart.title: your gut feels your stress before you do
coldStart.content: the fight-or-flight response shuts down digestion within minutes of a stress trigger. if you often have digestive issues during stressful periods, tracking both will make the pattern undeniable.

coldStart.title: your body sends signals every day
coldStart.content: sleep, energy, mood, focus — they're not random. they follow patterns that research has mapped for decades. meridian will find your personal version of these patterns. for now, here's what science knows about the connections between them.

coldStart.title: what to expect
coldStart.content: day 1-6: log daily, see population-level science about your signals. day 7: your first personal connections emerge. day 14: predictions begin. day 30+: deep pattern recognition. every log makes the picture clearer.

coldStart.title: science-backed sleep advice
coldStart.content: consistent sleep timing matters more than duration. keep your bedroom cool (65-68°F). avoid screens 30 minutes before bed. caffeine's half-life is 5-6 hours — an afternoon coffee is still 25% active at midnight.

coldStart.title: science-backed energy advice
coldStart.content: the three pillars of energy: sleep (fuel), exercise (generator), and nutrition (sustained release). a 10-minute walk boosts energy for 2 hours. blood sugar stability prevents crashes — eat every 4-5 hours with protein.

coldStart.title: science-backed mood advice
coldStart.content: morning sunlight, exercise, and social connection are the three most evidence-based mood interventions. even 5 minutes of outdoor time improves mood measurably. sleep deprivation amplifies negative emotions by 60%.
coldStart.source: Yoo et al. 2007; Chekroud et al. 2018; Thayer 1989

coldStart.title: science-backed focus advice
coldStart.content: sustained focus cycles last about 90 minutes. work in focused blocks of 25-50 minutes with breaks. your phone in another room improves focus more than any app or technique. deep work capacity: about 4 hours/day.

coldStart.title: exercisers sleep 20-30% more deeply
coldStart.content: regular exercise increases deep slow-wave sleep — the most physically restorative phase. the effect builds over 2-4 weeks. your data will show whether this holds for you.

coldStart.title: a little stress helps focus; a lot destroys it
coldStart.content: the Yerkes-Dodson law: performance peaks at moderate arousal. too little stress = boredom, too much = anxiety. your data will reveal your personal sweet spot.

coldStart.title: mood and energy share the same fuel
coldStart.content: dopamine powers both motivation (energy) and reward (mood). they usually move together. your data will show how tightly yours are linked.

coldStart.title: 50% of headaches are predictable with enough data
coldStart.content: most headaches have 2-3 consistent triggers. the most common: sleep changes, stress, dehydration, and skipped meals. with 2-4 weeks of data, meridian can identify your personal triggers.

coldStart.title: exercise before learning doubles retention
coldStart.content: the Naperville school study: students who exercised before class dramatically improved academic performance. BDNF from exercise enhances the brain's ability to form new connections.

coldStart.title: your signals are more connected than you think
coldStart.content: sleep affects energy, energy affects exercise, exercise affects mood, mood affects stress, stress affects sleep. these aren't separate systems — they're one interconnected network. that's why meridian uses a network graph.

coldStart.title: exercise is the most reliable mood booster
coldStart.content: a meta-analysis of 1.2 million adults found that exercisers had 43% fewer days of poor mental health per month. even 15 minutes of running or 45 minutes of walking significantly reduces depression risk. the mood boost kicks in within 30 minutes and lasts 12-24 hours.
coldStart.source: Chekroud et al. 2018, The Lancet Psychiatry

coldStart.title: stress and sleep feed each other
coldStart.content: stress raises cortisol, which blocks sleep onset. poor sleep raises cortisol the next day, increasing stress reactivity. this creates a vicious cycle — but breaking it at either end works. one night of good sleep reduces next-day cortisol by 25-30%. one day of managed stress improves that night's sleep onset by 20 minutes on average.
coldStart.source: Hirotsu et al. 2015; Sleep Medicine Reviews

coldStart.title: focus degrades linearly with sleep loss
coldStart.content: each hour of sleep below 7 hours reduces next-day cognitive performance by ~15%. at 6 hours, you're operating at ~85%. at 5 hours, ~70%. at 4 hours, ~55%. the insidious part: subjective sleepiness plateaus after a few days, so you stop noticing the decline even as performance keeps dropping.
coldStart.source: Van Dongen et al. 2003; Sleep Journal; Belenky et al. 2003

coldStart.title: the energy paradox: spending creates more
coldStart.content: counterintuitively, exercise increases rather than decreases overall energy. a University of Georgia meta-analysis found that regular exercise reduces fatigue by 65% and increases energy by 20% compared to sedentary controls. the effect is stronger than stimulant medications for chronic fatigue.

coldStart.title: sleep under 6h doubles headache risk
coldStart.content: research shows sleeping less than 6 hours increases next-day headache probability by 2x. but oversleeping (9+ hours) also increases risk by 1.5x. the sweet spot for headache prevention is consistently 7-8 hours. irregular sleep schedules are an additional risk factor independent of duration.
coldStart.source: Ødegård et al. 2011; Cephalalgia; American Headache Society

generalInsights.title: sleep is your keystone signal
generalInsights.text: in your data, sleep connects to more signals than any other. improving sleep tends to improve everything downstream — energy, mood, focus. it's the single highest-leverage intervention.

generalInsights.title: exercise boosts everything
generalInsights.text: your exercise data shows positive connections to [count] other signals. this matches population-level research — exercise is the closest thing to a universal health intervention.

generalInsights.title: stress is the disruptor
generalInsights.text: high stress in your data correlates with worse outcomes across multiple signals. stress disrupts sleep, energy, focus, mood, and digestion through cortisol and inflammatory pathways.

generalInsights.title: the virtuous cycle
generalInsights.text: your data shows a positive feedback loop: good sleep → more energy → more likely to exercise → better mood → better sleep. when one link improves, the whole chain lifts.

generalInsights.title: the spiral to watch
generalInsights.text: poor sleep → low energy → no exercise → stress → worse sleep. your data shows this pattern too. the key is to intervene on any single link — usually sleep or exercise is easiest.

generalInsights.title: consistency beats intensity
generalInsights.text: in your data, consistent moderate patterns outperform sporadic extremes. 7 hours of sleep every night beats alternating 5 and 9. regular walking beats occasional intense workouts. your body prefers rhythm.

generalInsights.title: your weekend pattern
generalInsights.text: your signals shift on weekends — [description]. this 'social jet lag' effect is common but can be minimized by keeping sleep timing consistent.

generalInsights.title: seasonal patterns may emerge
generalInsights.text: as your data grows, you may notice seasonal shifts in mood and energy. reduced daylight hours in winter lower serotonin production. awareness is the first step to adaptation.

generalInsights.title: your data gets smarter
generalInsights.text: correlations at day 7 are preliminary. at day 30, they're reliable. at day 90, they're deeply personal. every log adds resolution to your body's unique pattern.
generalInsights.source: Statistical significance improves with sample size

generalInsights.title: find the upstream signal
generalInsights.text: when multiple signals are down simultaneously, there's usually one upstream cause. in your data, [signal] appears to be the root. fixing it may fix the others.

generalInsights.title: change one thing at a time
generalInsights.text: when trying to improve a pattern, change ONE variable for ONE week. if you change sleep, exercise, and diet simultaneously, you won't know which worked. your data will be clearest with isolated experiments.

generalInsights.title: your body is unique
generalInsights.text: population averages are starting points, not targets. some people thrive on 7 hours of sleep; others need 9. your personal data is more accurate than any general recommendation.

generalInsights.title: the weekend effect on your signals
generalInsights.content: most people show measurable differences in sleep, mood, and energy between weekdays and weekends. weekend sleep is typically 30-60 minutes longer, mood 10-15% higher, but the Monday transition often creates a 'social jet lag' effect that impacts the first 2 days of the week.

generalInsights.title: your signals have seasons
generalInsights.content: energy, mood, and sleep quality all show seasonal patterns. in winter, reduced daylight suppresses serotonin and shifts melatonin timing, lowering mood and energy by 10-20% on average. this isn't weakness — it's biology. knowing this helps you adjust expectations and interventions seasonally.
generalInsights.source: Roecklein & Rohan 2005; Seasonal Affective Disorder research

generalInsights.title: keystone signals affect everything else
generalInsights.content: in your body's network, some signals are 'hubs' — they connect to many others. sleep is almost always the #1 hub, followed by exercise and stress. improving a hub signal creates cascading benefits: better sleep → better energy + mood + focus + fewer headaches. this is why meridian tracks connections, not just individual signals.
generalInsights.source: Duhigg 2012, The Power of Habit; network science principles
